design_philosophy.core_loop: One-on-one tournament fighting with progressive opponent difficulty; win matches through combat mastery to advance through bracket toward final boss Shao Kahn
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Execution-based combat against increasingly difficult AI opponents with pattern recognition required; players must learn character movesets and opponent tells to progress
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Martial arts master competing in interdimensional tournament; ability to execute stylish finishing moves (Fatalities, Friendships, Babalities) as power fantasy reward
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Game unapologetically embraces graphic violence and finishing moves as core appeal; deliberately positioned against Nintendo's censorship of first game; Friendships and Babalities show design humor but violence remains central identity

difficulty.lives_system: Match-based; players fight series of opponents consecutively, losing when they lose 2 rounds to any opponent
difficulty.continues_system: Arcade-style continue system for home port (as noted: 'didn't have to shove coins into your cartridge slot every ten seconds when fighting the ever-cheap bosses')
difficulty.checkpointing: No checkpoint system; must progress through entire tournament bracket from start; boss encounters (Shao Kahn, Kintaro as bodyguard) represent final difficulty spike
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Character selection affects approach; multiple playable characters with varied movesets allow different playstyle strategies

progression.unlock_mechanism: Hidden characters (Noob Saibot noted as secret character); secret finishing moves discovered through button combinations
replayability_hooks: Multiple playable characters with distinct movesets and special moves
Non-canonical character-specific endings for story mode
Multiple finishing moves per character (Fatalities, Friendships, Babalities, stage-specific finishers) encourage experimentation

world_building_techniques: Plot exposition: Shang Tsung's negotiation with Shao Kahn establishes tournament setup in Outworld setting
Character endings: Multiple non-canonical endings for each character completing story mode
Stage design: Visually distinct Outworld locations reinforce other-dimensional setting (Pit II with Stage Fatality backdrop showing Blaze figure)
Character roster expansion: Introduction of new characters (Kitana, Mileena, Kung Lao, Noob Saibot) expands mythos incrementally

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters represent significant difficulty spike as final tournament challenges; Shao Kahn positioned as ultimate antagonist with home field advantage; boss characters (Shao Kahn, Kintaro) restricted from using finishing moves against them, preventing player exploitation
notable_bosses: Shao Kahn (supreme ruler of Outworld, final tournament opponent, canonical final opponent for Liu Kang)
Kintaro (Shao Kahn's bodyguard, penultimate boss encounter)

faction_indicators: Earthrealm warriors vs. Outworld representatives (tournament-based faction system)
Shao Kahn's forces attempting to conquer Earthrealm through combat tournament
Thunder god Raiden as Earthrealm's protector and warrior gatherer
danger_signals: Shao Kahn deliberately moves tournament to Outworld to deny Earthrealm home advantage
Outworld framing as hostile, dangerous realm requiring warriors to travel away from protection
Restoring Shang Tsung's power indicates escalating threat level
environmental_storytelling: Stage design reflects Outworld nature (Pit II location with background figures, spikes in ceiling, acid pools)
Visual distinction between Earthrealm and Outworld tournament locations
Stage-specific Fatalities tie character victories to environmental hazards (abyss, spikes, acid)

sources_used: tcrf - The Cutting Room Floor Mortal Kombat II (SNES) article
wikipedia - Mortal Kombat II Wikipedia article
